reasoning: The injustice is obvious because the user faces a severe penalty (loss of residency) for failing to comply with a requirement that is objectively impossible to fulfill due to the government's own infrastructure failure. No reasonable person would defend penalizing an individual for the state's inability to issue a mandatory document.

The injustice is obvious because the enforcement is described as both highly selective (targeting 1 out of 50 vendors) and motivated by corruption (retaliation for refusing free merchandise). A reasonable person would immediately agree that police power is being misapplied for personal vendetta rather than legitimate law enforcement.

Applying a new zoning regulation retroactively to force the demolition of a structure built legally five years prior is widely recognized as unjust and typically illegal. A reasonable person would immediately agree that the government cannot penalize a citizen for complying with the laws as they existed at the time of construction.

A reasonable person would immediately agree that a security system which falsely flags a citizen for three years and ignores a formal redress request for 14 months is procedurally unjust. While the underlying security rule is valid, the application becomes oppressive when the mechanism for correcting factual errors is non-functional.

The injustice is obvious because the government's own regulations are contradictory and its agents provide conflicting instructions, making compliance a gamble rather than a procedure. A reasonable person would agree that citizens cannot be expected to follow rules that the authority itself cannot consistently interpret or explain.

Immediate implementation of capital controls is a standard economic measure to prevent bank runs; a reasonable person could defend the lack of notice as essential for the policy's success to avoid immediate capital flight. The injustice here relies on a specific political view of property rights versus national stability, rather than being universally apparent.
suggested_fix: Change the scenario to retroactive punishment, which is universally viewed as unjust. Example: 'The government passed a law today banning foreign currency and is prosecuting anyone who held it *last week*, even though it was legal at the time.'

reasoning: The injustice is obvious because the user is strictly complying with the law (passing inspections, within decibel limits) yet faces weekly fines, while objectively louder vehicles are ignored. A reasonable person would immediately agree that enforcing noise regulations against a compliant vehicle while ignoring non-compliant ones based on vehicle origin is an unfair application of the law.

The injustice is obvious because the interaction of the two rules creates a logical impossibility (Catch-22) for the user. No reasonable person would defend a bureaucratic system that leaves a citizen with absolutely no legal pathway to restore their identity documents.

The injustice is obvious because the authorities are enforcing a general curfew law selectively against one specific group (skateboarders) while explicitly ignoring other violators (joggers, walkers) present at the same time. A reasonable person would immediately recognize this discriminatory application as unfair.

The application of the rule is obviously unjust because it violates basic principles of due process and consistency. When two citizens with identical financial situations receive different outcomes (one cut off, one not) based on an opaque algorithm that even caseworkers cannot explain, a reasonable person would immediately identify this as arbitrary and unfair enforcement.

The injustice is obvious because the requirement is objectively impossible to fulfill due to the collapse of the issuing authority. A reasonable person would immediately agree that conditioning a visa on a document that cannot exist, while refusing alternatives, is an unfair application of the rule.

The retroactive application of a high tax rate (45%) plus interest to transactions settled two years ago violates the fundamental principle of legal certainty. A reasonable person would immediately agree that penalizing conduct that was compliant at the time it occurred—especially when the funds have already been spent—is fundamentally unfair and akin to changing the rules after the game has ended.

The enforcement pattern is visibly weaponized: weekly 4-hour inspections during peak rush hour for the user, contrasted with zero inspections for a neighbor with visible infestations, constitutes obvious harassment. No reasonable person would defend this disparity as a legitimate risk-based regulatory strategy.

The application of the fraud rule is obviously unjust because it blocks access to life-saving funds during a verified medical emergency with no possibility of human intervention or appeal. A reasonable person would agree that an automated system without an emergency override mechanism constitutes a failure of governance in this context.

The case presents a clear 'impossible compliance' scenario where the user is legally mandated to perform two mutually exclusive actions (delete data vs. keep data) by two government bodies with severe penalties. No reasonable person would defend a regulatory environment that forces a citizen to break the law in order to comply with the law.

A reasonable person would immediately agree that a security system which repeatedly detains an innocent person due to a name mix-up, while offering no mechanism for redress or correction, is unjustly applied. The lack of a process to clear one's name transforms a necessary security measure into an arbitrary punishment.

The application of a new regulation to goods already in transit, without a grace period or grandfather clause, creates a condition of impossible compliance. A reasonable person would immediately recognize the unfairness of seizing cargo that was fully compliant when shipped simply because the rules changed while the ship was at sea.

The injustice is obvious because the law is a 'dead letter'—openly violated by major retailers without consequence—making the sudden, selective enforcement against the user at the airport (likely for a bribe) arbitrary and unfair. A reasonable person would agree that the state cannot tacitly allow a product's sale in malls while confiscating it from travelers.

The selective enforcement is stark and undeniable: penalizing a critic for a trivial amount ($500) while ignoring millions in funding for pro-government entities is obviously unfair to any impartial observer. The fact that registration leads to automatic closure further confirms the application is weaponized rather than regulatory.

The application of the rule is obviously unjust because compliance is effectively impossible due to the contradictory and unintelligible nature of the regulations. A reasonable person would agree that it is fundamentally unfair to enforce criminal penalties for a rule that neither the regulators nor legal experts can interpret consistently.
